images.copyright: Great Barrier Reef from above, Queensland, Australia (© Francesco Riccardo Iacomino/Getty Images)
images.title: Underwater architecture
images.caption: Great Barrier Reef from above, Queensland, Australia
images.desc: One of the world's greatest natural wonders stretches through the turquoise waters of the Coral Sea off Australia's northeast shoreline. The Great Barrier Reef, running along the coast of Queensland, is the world's largest coral reef system. It is made up of nearly 3,000 individual reefs and hundreds of islands, spanning more than 2,253 kilometres.

images.copyright: Polar bear mother and cubs playing in Wapusk National Park, Manitoba (© Hao Jiang/Getty Images)
images.title: A bond that endures
images.caption: Happy Mother's Day!
images.desc: On Mother's Day, we honour the care, patience and protection that shape lives everywhere. In the wild, these qualities often stand out most where survival is hardest. Captured in Wapusk National Park in northern Manitoba, today's image shows a polar bear mother and her cubs sharing a playful moment on the Arctic tundra.

images.copyright: Skradinski Buk Waterfall in Krka National Park, Croatia (© Amazing Aerial Agency/Adobe Stock)
images.title: The architect of Krka
images.caption: Skradinski Buk Waterfall, Krka National Park, Croatia
images.desc: Water is the architect of Krka National Park in Croatia, shaping emerald pools and layered waterfalls along the Krka River. Today's image highlights Skradinski Buk, the park's most famous waterfall system, where multiple streams spill over travertine terraces in a dense forest near the village of Lozovac.

images.copyright: Tulips and cherry blossoms at the Rideau Canal, Ottawa, Ontario (© J Duquette/Getty Images)
images.title: When Ottawa turns tulip
images.caption: Canadian Tulip Festival, Ottawa
images.desc: It starts quietly—rows of tulips opening along the Rideau Canal as spring settles in. Then the story catches up. The Canadian Tulip Festival begins today, marking its 74th year, but its roots go back to wartime Europe. In 1945, after Canada helped liberate the Netherlands and hosted Princess Juliana during her exile, a gift of tulip bulbs arrived in Ottawa. That tradition didn't end; it returned each year, growing into today's festival.

images.copyright: Kofa National Wildlife Refuge, Arizona, United States (© Denis Tangney Jr/Getty Images)
images.title: Desert, drawn wide
images.caption: Kofa National Wildlife Refuge, Arizona, United States
images.desc: Stretching across the Sonoran Desert in southwestern Arizona, Kofa National Wildlife Refuge is a land of sharp peaks, wide skies and surprising life. The refuge was established in 1939 to protect desert bighorn sheep, whose sure-footed silhouettes still define the rugged landscape.

images.title: Plains under pressure
images.caption: Thunderstorm above the plains, Bulgaria
images.desc: Located on the eastern Balkan Peninsula, Bulgaria sits at the crossroads of Europe and Asia, where ancient cultures, fertile landscapes and shifting climates converge.

images.copyright: A majestic bull moose foraging through the green undergrowth, Quebec (© pchoui/Getty Images)
images.title: In step with the forest
images.caption: A bull moose foraging through the green undergrowth, Quebec
images.desc: Not one to hurry, a bull moose moves with steady purpose—and for good reason. As part of the North American moose population native to Canada, these animals are well adapted to long winters and short growing seasons. Their diet shifts with availability: in summer, they browse fresh leaves, shrubs and aquatic plants; in winter, they rely more on woody material like twigs and bark.

images.copyright: Ksar Ouled Soltane, Tataouine district in southern Tunisia (© Dark_Eni/Getty Images Plus)
images.title: Sci‑fi, Earth‑made
images.caption: Star Wars Day
images.desc: May the 4th be with you! On Star Wars Day, reality cosplays as science fiction and dares you to tell the difference. Our image takes us to Ksar Ouled Soltane in Tunisia's Tataouine district, where stacked clay granaries rise like sand‑coloured fortresses. Dating back to the 15th century, this storehouse complex was built by Berber communities to protect harvests from heat, theft and time. Function first. Beauty as a side effect.
